MOTION TO QUASH SUBPOENAS
Off Calendar Jun-25-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. HANNAH OXLEY, Esq. of Pearce Lewis LLP (tel: 415-964-5225) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. AMY FREELAND, Esq. of Sims, Lawrence & Broghammer (tel: 916-797-8881) for Defendants City and County of San Francisco and Shireen McSpadden.
Atty. CARMEN GUTMAN, Esq. of Cholokian and Associates (tel: 650-871-9544) for Defendants Wehope and Alicia Garcia.
Atty. MAIHUONG TA, Esq. of Kronenberg Law PC (tel: 510-254-6767) for Defendant Rahsaun Horn.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF DEBBIE JOSE's MOTION TO QUASH SUBPOENAS.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with modifications.

Plaintiff's motion to quash is granted.

Although personal injury plaintiffs implicitly waive their right to privacy, the waiver is not unlimited. (Davis v. Superior Ct. (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1008, 1014 [holding that an implicit waiver of the right of privacy will encompass discovery "directly relevant" to the plaintiff's claims].) "[P]laintiffs . . . may not withhold information which relates to any physical or mental condition which they have put in issue by bringing th[e] lawsuit." (Britt v. Superior Ct. (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 864.)

Here, defendant's subpoenas are over broad as they seek "any and all" medical and mental health records with no time limitation. Plaintiff's records regarding her prior trauma and drug and alcohol treatment are not "directly relevant" to this lawsuit.

Order signed.

Judge: John M. True; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Court reporter: Raquel Sharp, CSR # 10619 (tel: 925-945-9888 / email: raquel@transcriptlegal.com). (301/JMT)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-01-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Hearing re: DEFENDANTS SHAREEN MCSPADDEN, IN HER INDIVIDUAL CAPACITY AS DIRECTOR OF THE DEPARTMENT OF HOMELESSNESS AND SUPPORTIVE HOUSING, CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants City and County of San Francisco and Shareen McSpadden's motion to strike punitive damages is granted.

First, Government Code section 818 bars punitive damages against the City only. (Runyon v. Superior Ct. (1986) 187 Cal. App. 3d 878, 881 ["Section 818 setting forth the immunity from punitive damages expressly pertains only to public entities. There is no corresponding immunity provision pertaining to public employees. Thus, a public employee may be liable for punitive damages like a private person but unlike a public entity which is specifically immune"].)

Second, Jose fails to allege ultimate facts of McSpadden's oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294.) The gravamen of Jose's complaint with respect to McSpadden is that McSpadden negligently hired and retained the non-profit WeHope and failed to supervise WeHope when it hired the assailant. (Compl. para. 61, 69.) These allegations of negligence do not support the inference that McSpadden hired WeHope with malice towards the shelter residents, or to oppress or commit fraud upon them.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. CRC 3.1312 compliance is required.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff: Park. (301/RCE)
